title: Needle-felted owl
subtitle: A compact barn-owl figure with disc face and surface feather detail
excerpt: Needle-felt a compact teardrop body in cream and warm-grey merino, shape the characteristic flat facial disc, and add wing and feather markings with surface colour work. The barn owl suits beginners: the body shape is simple, and its naturally pale colouring means minor unevenness blends into the texture.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted bird figures in the barn-owl form are a standard teaching piece in UK guild workshops. The flat facial disc and teardrop body are the standard construction used across the field.

content.text: The barn owl is built from a
in cream merino, with warm-buff and grey-brown surface markings and a separately needled

attached to the front. The finished owl is approximately 9 cm tall and 5 cm wide at the shoulder. It stands on two small twisted-wire legs if you want it perched, or simply sits with its flat base on a surface.

attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: Take 14 g of cream roving and roll it into a compact cylinder. Begin needling on the foam mat with a size 38 needle, rotating the form and working from the base upward. As the base compacts, ease the top into a point by needling the upper section more than the lower, creating the teardrop shape. Work until the form is firm throughout and holds the teardrop profile without pressure.
Step 2: Add back and wing markings

Lay a thin veil of warm-buff roving over the upper back and work it in with the size 38 needle using light strokes. Work from the edges of the buff area inward to blend the boundary. Add scattered grey-brown dots across the wing area by placing tiny pinches of roving and needling each pinch from its centre outward.
Step 3: Make and attach the facial disc

Take 4 g of cream roving and needle it into a flat, roughly heart-shaped pad about 3 cm across on the foam mat. Needle the edges to define the heart shape. Once firm, hold the disc against the lower front of the head and needle through both layers to attach, working around the edge of the disc first, then the centre.

Using the size 40 fine needle, needle two small rounds of black roving onto the facial disc, about 1 cm apart and centred. For the beak, take a tiny pinch of pale yellow-cream roving, needle it into a small pointed triangle on the mat, then attach at the centre of the disc between the eyes.

items.fix: Work additional strokes through the disc into the head, concentrating on the centre rather than only the edges.
items.cause: The disc was not needled through both layers firmly enough when attaching
items.problem: The facial disc falls off or detaches

items.fix: Use less buff roving as a veil, and needle only within the buff area, not across the edge.
items.cause: Too much roving applied or strokes worked across the colour boundary
items.problem: The buff back colour bleeds into the cream chest

items.fix: Needle the upper third more than the lower to compress the top more, drawing it into a point.
items.cause: The upper section was compacted at the same rate as the lower
items.problem: The body is too short and wide rather than teardrop-shaped
